text:
You are an AI programming assistant named Alfred. Follow the user's requirements carefully and to the letter. First, think step-by-step and describe your plan for what to build in pseudocode, written out in great detail. Then, output the code in a single code block. Minimize any other prose.

```sty LATEX_CONF_2024_10_02/preamble.sty
% --------------------
% Color theme settings
% --------------------
\usetheme{default}
\usecolortheme{spruce}

% -----------------
% Language settings
% -----------------
\usepackage[style=verbose]{biblatex}
\usepackage[greek, english, french]{babel} % Using different languages


% ------------
% Bibliography
% ------------
\addbibresource{refs.bib}
\renewcommand{\footnotesize}{\tiny} % resize foot citation

% --------------
% Math & Physics
% --------------
\usepackage{physics}
\usepackage{mathtools}
\usepackage{listings}
\usepackage{dsfont}

% -------------
% Font settings
% -------------
\setbeamerfont{framesubtitle}{size=\small}
\usefonttheme{serif} % default family is serif
\usepackage[mono,vvarbb,upint]{notomath} % Font

% ------------
% TOC settings
% ------------
\setbeamercolor{section in toc}{fg=black}
\setbeamertemplate{section in toc}[square]
\setbeamertemplate{section in toc}{%
  {\color{spruce_medium}\rule{6pt}{6pt}}~\inserttocsection\par}
\setbeamertemplate{subsection in toc}{%
  \vspace{0.25em}\hspace{2em}{\color{spruce_medium}\rule[0.3ex]{3pt}{3pt}}~\inserttocsubsection\par}

% ----------------------
% Global frames settings
% ----------------------
\beamertemplatenavigationsymbolsempty
\setbeamercolor{section number projected}{fg=black, bg=spruce_light}

% Frame number
\addtobeamertemplate{navigation symbols}{}{
    \usebeamerfont{footline}
    \usebeamercolor[hard_green]{footline}
    \hspace{1em}
    \insertframenumber/\inserttotalframenumber
}

% ------------------------
% Colored Boxes and blocks
% ------------------------
\usepackage{xcolor}
\usepackage{tcolorbox}

% Custom colors
\definecolor{sweet_refs}{RGB}{121, 123, 202} % #797bca
\definecolor{spruce_light}{RGB}{229, 240, 235} % #e5f0eb
\definecolor{spruce_medium}{RGB}{115, 151, 134} % #739786
\definecolor{spruce_dark}{RGB}{0, 61, 32} % #003d20
\definecolor{hard_green}{RGB}{0, 128, 64} % #66b27b
\definecolor{sweet_brown}{RGB}{197, 180, 160} % #bba790

% Colored blocks
\newenvironment{noteblock}[1]{
    \tcolorbox[noparskip, colback=white,
    colframe=sweet_brown, boxrule=1pt, arc=1pt, title=#1, coltitle=black]}
    {\endtcolorbox
}
```

Please help me with the toc's squares. I want the sections to be numbered using the "square" style with my custom color: spruce_medium
Pseudocode for modifying the LaTeX `.sty` file:
1. Begin by locating where the `table of contents (TOC)` settings are configured for section numbers.
2. Modify the existing template for `section in toc` to include automatic numbering for sections within a square shape.
3. Ensure that the square uses the `spruce_medium` color as desired for both framing and filling the square.
4. Adjust the section numbering logic to format as `<section number>.`, preceding the section title.
5. Apply the changes while making sure other styling remains unaffected, specifically maintaining custom color and style settings throughout the template.

Here is the modified LaTeX snippet as per the requirements for updating the square style for section numbers in the table of contents using the color `spruce_medium`:

```latex
% --------------------
% Color theme settings
% --------------------
\usetheme{default}
\usecolortheme{spruce}

% -----------------
% Language settings
% -----------------
\usepackage[style=verbose]{biblatex}
\usepackage[greek, english, french]{babel} % Using different languages

% ------------
% Bibliography
% ------------
\addbibresource{refs.bib}
\renewcommand{\footnotesize}{\tiny} % resize foot citation

% --------------
% Math & Physics
% --------------
\usepackage{physics}
\usepackage{mathtools}
\usepackage{listings}
\usepackage{dsfont}

% -------------
% Font settings
% -------------
\setbeamerfont{framesubtitle}{size=\small}
\usefonttheme{serif} % default family is serif
\usepackage[mono,vvarbb,upint]{notomath} % Font

% ------------
% TOC settings
% ------------
\setbeamercolor{section in toc}{fg=black}
\setbeamertemplate{section in toc}[square]
\setbeamertemplate{section in toc}{%
  \color{spruce_medium}\usebeamercolor[fg]{item projected}\setbeamerfont{item projected}{\inserttocsectionnumber.}
  {\color{spruce_medium}\rule{6pt}{6pt}}~\inserttocsection\par}
\setbeamertemplate{subsection in toc}{%
  \vspace{0.25em}\hspace{2em}{\color{spruce_medium}\rule[0.3ex]{3pt}{3pt}}~\inserttocsubsection\par}

% ----------------------
% Global frames settings
% ----------------------
\beamertemplatenavigationsymbolsempty
\setbeamercolor{section number projected}{bg=spruce_medium, fg=white}

% Frame number
\addtobeamertemplate{navigation symbols}{}{
    \usebeamerfont{footline}
    \usebeamercolor[hard_green]{footline}
    \hspace{1em}
    \insertframenumber/\inserttotalframenumber
}

% ------------------------
% Colored Boxes and blocks
% ------------------------
\usepackage{xcolor}
\usepackage{tcolorbox}

% Custom colors
\definecolor{sweet_refs}{RGB}{121, 123, 202} % #797bca
\definecolor{spruce_light}{RGB}{229, 240, 235} % #e5f0eb
\definecolor{spruce_medium}{RGB}{115, 151, 134} % #739786
\definecolor{spruce_dark}{RGB}{0, 61, 32} % #003d20
\definecolor{hard_green}{RGB}{0, 128, 64} % #66b27b
\definecolor{sweet_brown}{RGB}{197, 180, 160} % #bba790

% Colored blocks
\newenvironment{noteblock}[1]{
    \tcolorbox[noparskip, colback=white,
    colframe=sweet_brown, boxrule=1pt, arc=1pt, title=#1, coltitle=black]}
    {\endtcolorbox}
```
This part: \setbeamertemplate{section in toc}{%
  \color{spruce_medium}\usebeamercolor[fg]{item projected}\setbeamerfont{item projected}{\inserttocsectionnumber.}
  {\color{spruce_medium}\rule{6pt}{6pt}}~\inserttocsection\par}
  
does not work properly...
Let’s make the necessary adjustments to the LaTeX code to format the numbered squares in the TOC as per your specifications. We'll update the templates correctly to ensure that the numbering is efficiently displayed and only the section number is colored and styled within a square, maintaining the color theme as `spruce_medium`.

Here is the corrected LaTeX snippet:

```latex
% TOC settings
\setbeamercolor{section in toc}{fg=black}
\setbeamercolor{section number projected}{bg=spruce_medium, fg=white} % setting the color for the section numbers within toc
\setbeamertemplate{section in toc}{
  \setlength{\fboxsep}{0pt}
  \colorbox{spruce_medium}{\color{white}\bfseries\strut\space\inserttocsectionnumber.\space}%
  \hspace{1em}\inserttocsection\par
}
\setbeamertemplate{subsection in toc}{
  \vspace{0.25em}% Vertical space before each subsection
  \hspace{2em}% Horizontal space before each subsection
  \color{spruce_medium}\rule[0.3ex]{3pt}{3pt}%
  ~\inserttocsubsection\par% Insert subsection
}
```
Explanation:
1. **Section Numbering in Squares**: The section number for each section in the TOC is wrapped within a `colorbox` which colors the background using `spruce_medium`. The number itself is colored white for contrast against the background.
2. **Subsection Formatting**: Maintains your earlier design but properly uses spacing and colors from variable definitions to ensure style consistency.

This correction in the preamble `.sty` file should display the section numbers within colored squares appropriately while maintaining the document style and aesthetics.
Now I want the sections color to be specific. How can I do this...
To set a specific color for the section titles in the table of contents, you will adjust the color properties within the template for sections in the table of contents (TOC). This involves explicitly setting the text color right before inserting the section title. Let's modify the code to achieve this.

Here's how you can modify the LaTeX snippet:

```latex
% TOC settings
\setbeamercolor{section in toc}{fg=black} % Default color for section text

% Define the color for section titles specifically
\definecolor{section_title_color}{RGB}{115, 151, 134} % Using spruce_medium color or any other specific color you choose

\setbeamertemplate{section in toc}{
  \setlength{\fboxsep}{0pt}
  \colorbox{spruce_medium}{\color{white}\bfseries\strut\space\inserttocsectionnumber.\space}%
  \hspace{1em}\color{section_title_color}\inserttocsection\par
}

\setbeamertemplate{subsection in toc}{
  \vspace{0.25em}% Vertical space before each subsection
  \hspace{2em}% Horizontal space before each subsection
  \color{spruce_medium}\rule[0.3ex]{3pt}{3pt}%
  ~\inserttocsubsection\par% Insert subsection content
}
```

**Explanation:**
1. **Color Definition**: A color `section_title_color` is defined using RGB values. Here, I've used the RGB for `spruce_medium` as an example. You can replace it with any color you prefer.
2. **Section Title Formatting**: In the section TOC template, the `\color` command sets the text color to `section_title_color` right before inserting the section title with `\inserttocsection`. 

By modifying these aspects, you ensure that the titles of the sections are exactly the color you require, distinct from other textual elements in your TOC if necessary.
Not in the TOC, in the frames and title page
To set specific colors for the section titles in the frames and on the title page within a Beamer presentation, you need to adjust the settings related to the Beamer template for frame titles and perhaps redefine some additional Beamer elements. Here’s how to modify your LaTeX preamble to achieve that customization.

**Code Example with Specific Colors for Sections in Frames and Title Page:**

```latex
% Custom colors
\definecolor{section_title_color}{RGB}{115, 151, 134} % Using spruce_medium color or any specific color

% Setting color for section titles in frames
\setbeamercolor{frametitle}{fg=section_title_color, bg=white}

% Setting color for title and subtitle in title page
\setbeamercolor{title}{fg=section_title_color, bg=white}
\setbeamercolor{subtitle}{fg=section_title_color, bg=white}

% Customizing the section page (Optional)
\setbeamertemplate{section page}
{
    \thispagestyle{empty}
    \begin{center}
        {\usebeamerfont{section name}\usebeamercolor[fg]{section name}\insertsectionheadnumber}
        \vskip1em\par
        \begin{beamercolorbox}[sep=12pt,center]{section title}
            \usebeamerfont{section title}\insertsection\par
        \end{beamercolorbox}
    \end{center}
}

% If you also want to customize how Sections look at the start of each section:
\AtBeginSection[]{
  \frame{\sectionpage}
}

```

**Explanation of the Changes:**

1. **Defining Section Title Color:** The `section_title_color` is defined using RGB, allowing you to use a consistent color theme throughout the document.

2. **Frame Titles:** The `frametitle` colors are set to use `section_title_color` for the foreground (text color) and white for the background. This modifies the color of titles on individual content frames.

3. **Title Page Titles:** Both the `title` and `subtitle` in the title page settings are set similarly to use `section_title_color`. This ensures that your main presentation title and subtitle on the initial slide match this schema.

4. **Section Pages:** A new template for `section pages` (special slides that can appear at the beginning of each new section for clarity or division of topics) is defined, optionally, to stylize these dividers. This template centers and colors the section title in `section_title_color`.

5. **Automatic Section Page Insertion:** The `\AtBeginSection[]` command automatically adds a section page at the beginning of each section if you choose to have this feature for better section demarcation in your presentation.

This implementation will provide a consistent and themed visual design to your presentation, focusing on the section titles wherever they appear across frames and the title page, also optionally affecting new section introductions.

Give me a color a little bit lighter than this: \definecolor{spruce_medium}{RGB}{115, 151, 134} % #739786
To create a color lighter than the `spruce_medium`, you can increment the RGB values slightly within a reasonable range to avoid losing the hue's character. A lighter version could be achieved by increasing each RGB component by about 10%. Here is a suggested lighter color:

```latex
\definecolor{lighter_spruce_medium}{RGB}{128, 166, 148} % Adjusted from {115, 151, 134}
```

This color maintains the original's hue but appears lighter, suitable for designs where a more subtle variation is required.
Even a little bit lighter
To make the color even lighter than the previous suggestion, while ensuring it remains in the same hue family, we can further increase the RGB values. Here's a modification to make it lighter:

```latex
\definecolor{lighter_spruce_medium}{RGB}{150, 180, 160} % Adjusted from {128, 166, 148}
```

This updated color enhances the brightness, presenting a lighter shade while keeping the essence of `spruce_medium`.
One last time
Certainly! We'll make the color even lighter by further boosting the RGB values for a more subtle appearance. Here's the adjustment:

```latex
\definecolor{lighter_spruce_medium}{RGB}{175, 200, 185} % Adjusted from {150, 180, 160}
```

This revised shade keeps the visual harmony of the original spruce medium hue while offering a significantly lighter version.
Based on this: \definecolor{spruce_medium}{RGB}{175, 200, 185} % #739786, create a vibrant green to make some important text colored during presentation
To create a vibrant green color starting from the `spruce_medium` version and to make it stand out for emphasizing important text during a presentation, you can adjust the `RGB` values to increase saturation and brightness, leaning towards a more vivid green. Here’s a suggested vibrant green:

```latex
\definecolor{vibrant_green}{RGB}{85, 215, 147} % A vibrant, lively green color
```

This color boosts the green component significantly while moderately adjusting the red and blue components to create a fresh, eye-catching green that is useful for highlights and important text.

I meant a darker vibrant
To achieve a darker yet vibrant green starting from the previously light `spruce_medium` color that also appears vibrant, we will adjust the RGB values to deepen the green while maintaining a vivid appearance. Here's how you can define a darker vibrant green:

```latex
\definecolor{vibrant_green_dark}{RGB}{60, 180, 135} % A rich, dark, vibrant green
```

This variant retains a strong green component but shifts towards darker tones, enhancing the depth while preserving the vibrancy, suitable for impactful text in presentations.

stronger
For an even stronger and darker shade of vibrant green that conveys more depth and intensity, we can further darken the green value and adjust the red and blue to ensure the vibrancy remains evident:

```latex
\definecolor{vibrant_green_strong}{RGB}{30, 150, 110} % A stronger, darker, vibrant green
```

This color choice reduces the brightness significantly while enhancing the overall appearance of depth, making it ideal for critical and striking presentation highlights.
Find a brown that could go with the \definecolor{lighter_spruce_medium}{RGB}{175, 200, 185}
To find a complementary brown that coordinates well with the `lighter_spruce_medium` shade (RGB 175, 200, 185), the brown should ideally have warm undertones to harmonize with the cooler green. Here’s a suggested brown:

```latex
\definecolor{complementary_brown}{RGB}{139, 119, 101} % A warm, earthy brown
```

This color offers a natural, earthy tone that complements the subdued green of `lighter_spruce_medium`, creating a balanced and pleasing color palette suitable for a professional or aesthetic presentation setting.
much lighter please...
To provide a much lighter shade of brown that complements and balances with the `lighter_spruce_medium` (RGB 175, 200, 185), you could consider a sandy or beige tone that maintains warmth but with a softer presence:

```latex
\definecolor{light_complementary_brown}{RGB}{202, 184, 166} % A light sandy brown
```

This light, sandy brown retains the warmth required to harmonize with the cool green while providing a more subtle and gentle visual contrast. This makes it highly suitable for designs that aim for a soft yet distinguished look.
maybe more pastel-ish
For a more pastel-like light brown that better suits the lighter and softer tones of the `lighter_spruce_medium` color, you might want something even more subdued and delicate. Here's a suggestion for a pastel brown:

```latex
\definecolor{pastel_brown}{RGB}{227, 212, 196} % A soft, pastel brown
```

This color offers a very light, nearly off-white shade of brown with just enough hint of warmth to feel cozy and inviting, complementing the pastel green without overwhelming it. Perfect for adding subtle accents in a harmoniously soft colored presentation.

What does this do: \usepackage{csquotes}

The LaTeX package `csquotes` is designed to help with the typesetting of quotations. It provides advanced facilities for inline and display quotations, and is particularly useful for managing language-dependent quoting styles, making it quite handy in multilingual documents. Here's a brief overview of what `csquotes` does:

1. **Quotation Marks Management**: `csquotes` automatically handles opening and closing quotations marks according to the language settings of the document or the specific language environment. This is especially beneficial in multilingual typesetting where different languages require different styles of quotation marks.

2. **Nested Quotations**: The package supports nested quotations, automatically changing quotation marks styles to fit nesting levels correctly. For example, in English, double quotation marks might switch to single quotation marks inside a nested quote.

3. **Integration with BibLaTeX**: `csquotes` integrates seamlessly with the `biblatex` package for better handling of quotations in bibliographic contexts.

4. **Customizable Styles**: The package allows styles of quotations to be customized and switched dynamically. You can define your own style or modify existing ones as needed.

5. **Block Quotations**: Beyond just inline quotes, `csquotes` also supports block quotations which are useful for longer excerpts that need to be displayed distinctly from the main text.

6. **Commands for Quotations**: It provides commands such as `\textquote`, `\blockquote`, `\foreignquote`, and `\hyphenquote` for different quoting needs, like quoting foreign text or handling hyphenation properly within quotes.

By using `csquotes`, users can ensure that their document adheres to the typographic conventions for quotations more accurately and appropriately, adjusting dynamically to the document’s language settings.
can you do the light_spruce_medium a little lighter
To make the `lighter_spruce_medium` color even lighter, which already has RGB values of \(175, 200, 185\), I will adjust the RGB values further towards white. Here's a suggestion for an even lighter shade:

```latex
\definecolor{lighter_spruce_medium}{RGB}{195, 215, 205} % An even lighter shade of spruce medium
```

This adjustment increases each component, pushing the color closer to pastel and bringing it significantly nearer to a lighter spectrum while still maintaining a hint of the original hue. This lighter shade would be soothing and visually appealing, ideal for backgrounds or soft highlights in your document.
Find a pastel-ish blue or lila to go with the brown and greens
To complement the suggested colors, particularly the pastel brown (RGB 227, 212, 196) and the lighter spruce medium (RGB 195, 215, 205), I'll propose a pastel blue or lilac (lila) that harmonizes well with these selections. Here’s a recommendation for a color that subtly blends with both while adding a gentle visual contrast:

```latex
\definecolor{pastel_blue_lilac}{RGB}{183, 198, 220} % A soft, pastel blue-lilac
```

This color choice provides a cool tone that softly contrasts the warmth of the brown and complements the coolness of the green. It's light enough to maintain the pastel theme, providing a soothing visual experience which is ideal for background colors, highlights, or decorative elements in your document or presentation.
much darker please
For a darker shade of the pastel blue-lilac that still complements the light brown and lighter spruce medium, while offering more visual contrast, consider this adjusted color:

```latex
\definecolor{darker_blue_lilac}{RGB}{102, 122, 162} % A darker shade of blue-lilac
```

This color deepens the blue and lilac hues, enhancing the vibrancy and providing a sturdier visual anchor among the lighter pastel shades. This darker tone is suitable for accents or focal text that stands out against the gentler background colors.
In my ./.config/latexmkrc, how do I setup a default pdf viewer?
To configure a default PDF viewer in your `latexmk` configuration file (typically named `latexmkrc`), you'll need to set the `$pdf_previewer` variable to the command that opens your preferred PDF viewer. Here’s how you can do it, tailored to different operating systems.

### For General (Cross-Platform):
You can set a universal command if your PDF viewer is accessible by a consistent command across platforms:
```perl
$pdf_previewer = 'start viewer_command';
```
Replace `viewer_command` with the actual command to open your viewer. For instance, if you are using Adobe Reader, it could be `AcroRd32.exe` on Windows.

### Specific Examples:

**1. On Windows:**
If using Adobe Acrobat Reader, you might configure it like this:
```perl
$pdf_previewer = 'start acrord32 %O %S';
```

**2. On macOS:**
For using Preview or another default application:
```perl
$pdf_previewer = 'open -a Preview %O %S';
```
For explicitly using another application like Skim:
```perl
$pdf_previewer = 'open -a Skim %O %S';
```

**3. On Linux:**
Using a typical viewer like Evince:
```perl
$pdf_previewer = 'evince %O %S';
```
Or for Okular:
```perl
$pdf_previewer = 'okular %O %S';
```

### Additional Configuration Notes:
- `%O` refers to options passed to the viewer.
- `%S` refers to the PDF file name.

### Example full configuration (for Windows using SumatraPDF as an example):
```perl
$pdf_previewer = 'start SumatraPDF %O %S';
```

### To Place in `latexmkrc`:
Put any of these lines directly into your `.latexmkrc` file located typically in your home directory or `.config` folder. Ensure there are no conflicting settings for `$pdf_previewer` elsewhere in the file.

After setting this, `latexmk` will automatically use the specified viewer to open the generated PDFs. Make sure that the path to your viewer is correct and that it operates as expected from the command line or terminal.
What is this: \beamertemplatenavigationsymbolsempty

The LaTeX command `\beamertemplatenavigationsymbolsempty` is used within Beamer presentations, a popular class for creating slides and presentations in LaTeX. This specific command is relevant when you want to modify the appearance of your Beamer slides.

### Purpose:
- **Removing Navigation Symbols**: `\beamertemplatenavigationsymbolsempty` sets the Beamer template for navigation symbols to empty, effectively removing the navigation symbols from the presentation slides.

### Context:
In Beamer, navigation symbols are small icons (arrows, frame circles, etc.) that usually appear in the footer of slides. These symbols are interactive and allow the viewer to navigate through the slides or sections directly by clicking on them.

### Usage Scenario:
- **Cleaner Slide Appearance**: Some users prefer a minimalist design or find the navigation symbols visually distracting or unnecessary, especially in professional or academic presentations where a clean look is desired.
- **Custom Navigation Controls**: When creating custom footers or redefining navigation interfaces within the presentation.

### Example Usage:

```latex
\documentclass{beamer}

% Remove default navigation bar
\beamertemplatenavigationsymbolsempty

\begin{document}

\begin{frame}
\frametitle{Sample Frame}
This is a text in a sample frame.
\end{frame}

\end{document}
```

In this example, the presentation will not display the typical navigation symbols on any of the slides, providing a cleaner view. This is particularly useful for printed versions of slides, or when the presenter prefers to use keyboard or pointer controls for slide navigation during a presentation.
traduce in english: % Exemple de références, aucune n'est réellement utilisée
% dans le document
The translation of the French comment into English is:

```plaintext
% Example of references, none are actually used

```
